key_insight: Tuning the oxygen/fuel ratio in HVOF spraying strongly changes the microstructure and performance of equiatomic CoCrFeNiMn high-entropy alloy coatings, with one regime delivering the best hardness and wear resistance.
engineering_relevance: This work shows that CoCrFeNiMn HEO coatings can be engineered as durable protective layers for components operating under severe wear and extreme service conditions.
method: Gas-atomized CoCrFeNiMn powder was deposited by HVOF using kerosene fuel and varying oxygen/fuel ratios.
Phase and microstructure were characterized with XRD, SEM, and EDS.
Mechanical and tribological performance were evaluated via microhardness, elastic modulus, and wear testing, then compared with Scheil-based thermodynamic predictions.
evidence_signal: The best-performing HVOF regime achieved a maximum microhardness of 783.8 HV and a minimum wear rate of 7.45 × 10^-5 mm3 × N^-1 × m^-1, consistent with non-equilibrium thermodynamic predictions.
watch_item: Performance appears sensitive to spray-parameter control, so coating quality may vary if oxygen/fuel ratio and thermal history are not tightly managed.
paper_title: Microstructure and Mechanical Properties of Equiatomic CoCrFeNiMn High-Entropy Alloy Coatings Fabricated by High-Velocity Oxygen Fuel Spraying
